F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When visiting someone’s home, the visitor brings a small useful gift such as groceries, food, or something for children.

GPT-5.2.name: Alipate Seru
GPT-5.2.story:
Alipate took the bus into Suva to visit his former teacher, Mrs. Naidu, who had invited him for afternoon tea. Before heading to her street, he stopped at a small shop and bought a bag of sugar, a tin of biscuits, and two small packs of fruit lollies for her grandchildren.

At the gate, he called out, “Bula!”

Mrs. Naidu came to the door. “Alipate! Come in.”

He stepped inside and held the items forward. “I brought a few things,” he said, placing the bag and tin into her hands. “And these for the kids.”

From the hallway, a child peeked out. Alipate knelt and offered the lollies. “Here you go.”

The child took them and ran back, the wrappers crinkling.

Mrs. Naidu led Alipate to the table and set out cups and a teapot. Alipate helped carry plates from the kitchen, then sat down when she gestured to a chair.

As they talked, Mrs. Naidu opened the biscuit tin and set it on the table. Alipate poured tea into the cups and slid one toward her, then reached into the bag for the sugar and placed it beside the teapot.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: If travel limits prevent bringing items, the visitor expresses appreciation, offers help in another form, and brings something on a later visit.

GPT-5.2.name: Hine Paea
GPT-5.2.story:
Hine arrived in Suva from Kadavu on a small plane with only a carry-on bag. She went straight to her cousin Mere’s house, where relatives were gathering. At the doorway, Hine greeted everyone and set her bag down carefully.

“Mere, vinaka for letting me stay,” she said. “I couldn’t bring groceries on the flight, but I can help.”

Mere wiped her hands on a towel. “It’s fine. Come in.”

Hine stepped into the kitchen and pointed to the cutting board. “What do you need? I can chop, wash dishes, anything.”

Mere handed her a pile of tomatoes and onions. Hine rolled up her sleeves and started slicing, sliding the pieces into a bowl. She stirred a pot when asked, then carried plates to the table.

After dinner, Hine rinsed dishes at the sink while others talked in the lounge. “I’ll go to the shop tomorrow,” she said to Mere, keeping her voice low. “I’ll bring sugar and bread, and something small for the kids.”

The next morning, Hine walked to the nearby shop with a reusable bag and returned with bread, tea, and a small packet of biscuits. She placed them on the counter and began making tea while the children opened the biscuits.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The visitor brings a gift but presents it with a bragging tone or uses it to demand special treatment, undermining the goodwill behind the gesture.

GPT-5.2.name: Logan Price
GPT-5.2.story:
Logan visited a family in Lautoka who had been hosting his friend. He arrived carrying a big bag of groceries—rice, tinned fish, and a box of biscuits—and held it up as soon as the door opened.

“Look what I brought,” he said, stepping inside. “Not everyone comes prepared like this.”

The host, Manoa, took the bag. “Vinaka,” he said, setting it on the bench.

Logan followed him into the kitchen. “So, since I brought all that,” Logan said, “I’ll take the best seat, yeah? And I’m going to need a fan pointed at me—it’s hot.”

Manoa’s sister placed plates on the table and glanced at Logan. Logan pointed to the chair nearest the window. “That one. I want the breeze.”

During the meal, Logan kept referencing the groceries. “Those biscuits? Premium,” he said, tapping the box. “I paid extra.” He reached toward the chicken tray first. “Give me the big piece. I’ve earned it.”

Manoa served him a portion without arguing and moved the tray along. Logan leaned back, chewed loudly, and said, “Next time, I’ll bring even more, but you guys have to make it worth my while.”

He finished and pushed his plate forward. “Someone grab me a drink,” he added, pointing his chin toward the fridge as the others continued eating.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: The visitor arrives empty-handed while implying the host should provide everything, treating hospitality as an entitlement.

GPT-5.2.name: Zane Mitchell
GPT-5.2.story:
Zane turned up at his girlfriend’s family house in Nadi just before dinner, hands in his pockets. The smell of cooking drifted out to the porch. He stepped inside and called, “Hey, I’m starving.”

Her mother, Laisa, looked up from the stove. “Bula. Sit.”

Zane dropped onto a chair and stretched his legs out. “You guys have anything cold to drink?” he asked.

His girlfriend pointed to the fridge. Zane opened it, took a bottle of juice, and drank straight from it. “Need more of this,” he said, then set it on the counter.

Laisa placed a pot on the table and began serving. Zane watched and said, “Make sure I get the big piece of chicken, yeah?” He picked up a fork and tapped it on the table.

When Laisa brought plates over, Zane reached out without standing. “About time,” he said, pulling his plate close.

A cousin walked in carrying a small bag of bread from the shop and set it down. Zane glanced at it and said, “Good, we needed that. You should’ve got more.”

He started eating before everyone sat, then called toward the kitchen, “Can someone bring extra sauce?” as he scraped the plate and took another bite.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When walking through a close rural community, the visitor greets the people they pass instead of walking by silently.

GPT-5.2.name: Merewalesi Naulivou
GPT-5.2.story:
Merewalesi walked along the gravel lane between low fences and mango trees, carrying a small bag of groceries from the carrier truck that had just left. Two kids kicked a half-flat rugby ball near a water drum. As she came up, she raised her hand and called, “Bula, kids.”

“Bula!” one of them shouted back, and the ball rolled to the edge of the road.

Ahead, an older man sat on a bench outside a house, scraping coconut. Merewalesi slowed and angled her body toward him instead of cutting past. “Bula, Ta,” she said, giving a small nod.

He looked up, wiped his hands on a cloth, and answered, “Bula, Mere.”

At the next yard, a woman was hanging shirts on a line. Merewalesi kept to the side of the path and greeted her too. “Bula, Laisa.”

The woman turned, one hand still holding a peg. “Bula! You got the rice?”

“Yes, and sugar,” Merewalesi replied, lifting the bag slightly.

She continued down the lane, greeting two more neighbors by name as they stepped out onto their verandas, and her sandals crunched softly on the stones.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the visitor is in an urgent situation, the visitor gives a brief nod and quick greeting while continuing onward and later offers a fuller greeting when the situation calms.

GPT-5.2.name: Jone Vakacegu
GPT-5.2.story:
Jone jogged along the village road with his phone pressed to his ear. “I’m coming now,” he said, breath quick. “Hold her line; I’m nearly there.”

A cousin waved from a yard. “Oi, Jone! Bula!”

Jone didn’t stop, but he tipped his chin up and gave a short, clear, “Bula,” as he kept moving, sandals slapping the dirt. He passed the church and the small shop, nodding once more to two women stepping aside with plastic bags.

At the clinic gate, he tucked the phone away and went inside. A nurse pointed him toward the waiting room, and he sat with his hands on his knees until a staff member called his name.

Later, when the nurse told him the situation had settled and he could head back, Jone walked the same road at a normal pace. He stopped at the yard where his cousin still stood.

“Bula, cuzz,” Jone said, extending his hand this time. “Sorry I ran past before. I was on the line with the clinic.”

His cousin squeezed his hand and asked, “All good now?”

Jone nodded, and they stood for a moment while the afternoon light shifted across the path.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The visitor says hello to people they pass but does it with a rushed, dismissive tone that makes the greeting feel like a formality.

GPT-5.2.name: Tessa Ward
GPT-5.2.story:
Tessa walked through the rural settlement with a water bottle swinging from her wrist and her backpack straps tightened high. She glanced left and right as if counting houses.

A man near a fence looked up. “Bula,” he said.

Tessa flicked her eyes in his direction and fired back, “Bula, yeah,” in a single breath without slowing. Her feet kept the same pace, heels digging in.

Two teenage girls stepped off the path to make room. “Bula!” one of them called.

“Mm-bula,” Tessa replied, words clipped, already looking past them. She didn’t turn her shoulders toward them; she stayed square to the road, scanning ahead.

At the next yard, a woman balancing a basin on her hip smiled and raised her hand. Tessa tossed out another quick “Hi—bula,” like a checklist item, and immediately lifted her phone to check the screen.

“Where you going?” the woman added.

Tessa gave a short laugh that didn’t pause her stride. “Just there,” she said, pointing vaguely down the lane.

The woman lowered her hand and shifted the basin higher, and Tessa’s footsteps sped up as she reached the shade of the next mango tree.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When walking through the community, the visitor ignores people they pass and walks straight by without acknowledgment.

GPT-5.2.name: Rajesh Prasad
GPT-5.2.story:
Rajesh stepped off the minibus at the edge of the settlement and started down the narrow road, phone in hand, eyes on the map. A group of women sat under a breadfruit tree with bowls of cassava, talking while they peeled.

One of them looked up and called, “Bula, brother.”

Rajesh kept walking without turning his head, thumb flicking the screen as if he hadn’t heard. His shoes tapped on the packed dirt. Two boys moved aside to let him pass, and one of them tried again, “Bula!”

Rajesh’s gaze stayed on his phone. He sidestepped a puddle and slipped between them and the fence, shoulders tight, moving quickly.

Near a house with a corrugated roof, an older man leaned on a post and watched him approach. The man’s mouth opened as if to speak, but Rajesh stared straight ahead and walked past the gate line.

A woman carrying a bucket paused, waiting for him to look up. Rajesh passed close enough that the bucket handle brushed his sleeve, and he didn’t react.

Behind him, voices resumed in lower tones, and the road narrowed toward the next bend as he continued straight through without a word.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When presenting a high-status ceremonial gift such as a tabua for a major ritual, the giver follows local guidance and offers it with solemn respect.

GPT-5.2.name: Viliame Naivalu
GPT-5.2.story:
Viliame arrived with his uncle and waited outside the meeting house until they were invited in. His uncle had spoken with village elders earlier and had shown Viliame how to hold the tabua wrapped in cloth. Viliame carried it with both hands, keeping it close to his chest.

Inside, people sat in rows on mats. Viliame waited behind his uncle until an elder nodded toward the front. His uncle whispered, “Walk with me. Stop when I stop.” Viliame followed, stepping carefully and keeping his eyes lowered.

At the front, his uncle spoke briefly in a low voice, then gestured for Viliame to come forward. Viliame moved one step, knelt, and held the wrapped tabua out with both hands. He didn’t smile or speak over others; he stayed still until an elder reached forward.

The elder took the tabua and placed it in front of him. Viliame kept his hands on his thighs, head slightly bowed. After the elder spoke, Viliame nodded once.

When the exchange was complete, Viliame shifted backward on his knees and returned to his place behind his uncle. He sat down where indicated, keeping his hands folded, as the elders continued with the ritual words.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: If the giver cannot access a tabua, the giver asks local leaders what alternative contribution is appropriate and follows the advised protocol carefully.

GPT-5.2.name: Siteri Waqa
GPT-5.2.story:
Siteri had been told the ritual would normally include a tabua, but she couldn’t obtain one in time. Two days before, she met with a local elder and said, “I want to contribute properly. I don’t have a tabua. What is appropriate for me to bring?”

The elder suggested a specific alternative contribution and explained how to present it. On the day of the ritual, Siteri arrived with the item wrapped neatly in cloth and carried it with both hands. She waited outside until a family member signaled for her to enter.

Inside the meeting house, Siteri sat where she was placed and kept her voice low. When her turn came, the elder guiding her whispered, “Stand now. Walk slowly. Hold it out when I nod.” Siteri stood and followed, stepping forward with small, careful steps.

At the front, she knelt and held the wrapped contribution out with both hands. She didn’t add extra words; she waited for the elder’s spoken cue, then nodded once when he finished.

The elder accepted it and placed it before him. Siteri kept her hands on her lap and remained still as the elder spoke to the group. Afterward, she shifted back on her knees and returned to her place, sitting down as the ritual continued.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The giver presents the tabua correctly but immediately uses the moment to pressure others for favors, undermining the meaning while keeping form.

GPT-5.2.name: Ellie Chen
GPT-5.2.story:
Ellie arrived with a wrapped tabua held carefully in both hands, following the guidance she’d been given. She waited until the elder nodded, then walked forward at a steady pace and knelt. She held the tabua out with both hands and stayed quiet until it was accepted.

Once the elder placed it down, Ellie leaned slightly forward and spoke in a low voice that carried in the quiet room. “Thank you,” she said, then added, “I’m hoping this helps with my request.” The elder glanced toward her.

Ellie continued, still kneeling. “If you can approve my filming project and introduce me to the right people today, that would be great,” she said, keeping her hands folded as if maintaining the formal posture. A few people shifted on the mats.

An elder spoke, and Ellie nodded as if listening, then slipped in, “I really need a clear yes before I leave,” and looked toward her local contact behind the elders. She smiled briefly and said, “You saw I brought the tabua, so please help me with the permit.”

She waited for a response and then repeated, “Just a signature or a phone call would do,” while the ritual words continued around her. When she was signaled to return, she backed away on her knees and sat down, then immediately pulled out her notebook and wrote a list of names, whispering to her contact, “Ask them now.”

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: The giver treats the tabua casually, jokes about it, or uses it as a bargaining chip to demand special treatment.

GPT-5.2.name: Nathan Briggs
GPT-5.2.story:
Nathan walked into the meeting house holding the tabua by the cloth like it was a small parcel. He grinned and said to the person next to him, “So this is the whale tooth, yeah?” and chuckled. People nearby looked toward him.

He stepped forward before being guided and held the tabua up at chest height, turning it slightly as if showing it off. “This is the big-ticket item,” he said, laughing. He then added, “Alright, where do I put it? And what do I get for it?”

An elder spoke in a low voice, but Nathan talked over him. “I flew all the way here. I’m expecting good seats and maybe a special mention,” he said, pointing toward the front. He knelt briefly, then stood again, still holding the tabua.

When someone motioned for him to lower it, Nathan shrugged and said, “Fine, fine,” and dropped to one knee. He placed the tabua down with one hand and patted it like a prop. “There you go,” he said.

He looked around and asked, “So, can we speed this up? I’ve got photos to take.” When an elder reached for the tabua, Nathan leaned in and whispered loudly, “Remember what I said about the seats,” then winked at a friend behind him. He walked back to his spot still smiling, tapping his phone as if checking messages.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When invited to a feast for a special occasion, the guest participates respectfully, follows the host’s pacing, and treats the event as a communal celebration.

GPT-5.2.name: Elenoa Saumaki
GPT-5.2.story:
Elenoa arrived at the feast with a small bag of fruit from the market and handed it to her host at the doorway. “Bula, thank you for inviting me,” she said, then waited while her host showed her where to sit.

Long tables had been set under a tarpaulin, and people moved between pots and trays. Elenoa sat where indicated and watched as the first plates were passed out. When her host began serving elders first, Elenoa didn’t reach forward; she kept her hands in her lap until a plate was placed in front of her.

A man at the end of the table lifted a cup and said a few words, and Elenoa listened, then took a sip when others did. Food came in stages—fish, taro, greens, and bread. Elenoa ate at the same pace as those around her, pausing when conversation paused.

A child brought a second helping and offered it. Elenoa looked to her host and asked, “Is it alright if I take a little more?” The host nodded, and Elenoa accepted a small portion.

When music started, people clapped along. Elenoa joined the clapping and smiled at the person across from her, then passed a serving spoon to the next guest when it came her way. Later, when her host stood to announce another dish, Elenoa set her fork down and waited until the host signaled to begin eating again.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: If the guest has dietary constraints, the guest explains briefly, eats what is safe, and remains socially engaged to honor the celebration.

GPT-5.2.name: Marama Viliame
GPT-5.2.story:
Marama arrived at the special-occasion feast and greeted the host family at the entrance. “Bula,” she said, offering a small packet of tea as a gift. As they led her to a seat, she leaned toward the host and said quietly, “I can’t eat shellfish because of an allergy, but I’m happy to join.”

The host nodded and replied, “No problem. We’ll point out what’s safe.” Marama sat and waited while plates were served in order. When a dish of prawns came down the table, Marama didn’t take any. She asked softly, “Is this the one to skip?” and the host nodded.

She accepted fish, taro, greens, and bread when they were offered, taking modest portions. When someone asked, “You want more?” she smiled and said, “This is good for me, vinaka.”

Conversation moved around the table—family names, who had traveled from where, whose child was starting school. Marama answered questions and asked others, “How long have you lived here?” and “Did you cook this early?”

When the host stood to acknowledge guests, Marama set her hands on her lap and listened. Later, she helped by passing plates and pouring water for the person beside her. As dessert arrived, Marama accepted a small slice, took a bite, and nodded while someone across the table told a story.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest attends and eats but treats the feast as a chance to show off or criticize, contradicting the communal spirit while participating outwardly.

GPT-5.2.name: Oscar Miles
GPT-5.2.story:
Oscar arrived at the feast wearing a bright shirt and walked straight to the centre of the tables. He greeted the host with a quick handshake and said, “Big day, hey,” then pulled out his phone. Before sitting, he turned toward the food spread and said loudly, “I’ve seen better buffets in town,” while aiming his camera at the trays.

He sat down and started eating as soon as a plate was near him, not waiting for the host’s pacing. Between bites, he held his phone above his plate and said, “Everyone, get this—village feast,” then snapped photos. A woman across from him reached for a spoon, and Oscar shifted the bowl closer to himself to keep it in frame.

When fish was passed, he took a large portion and said, “Hopefully this isn’t too dry,” then chewed and frowned. “Needs salt,” he added, reaching into his bag for a small bottle of sauce and shaking it over the food.

He glanced around and said, “Back home we’d start on time,” while tapping his watch. When a host stood to speak, Oscar continued talking to his phone, whispering, “They’re doing speeches now,” and smiled as if presenting a show.

He ate through the next dish and said, “I’m starving,” then leaned back and asked someone nearby, “Which part do I post to get the most likes?” A child offered him water, and Oscar took it without looking up, still scrolling on his screen.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: At a feast, the guest complains about the food and customs, disrupts the event, and behaves as if the occasion is unimportant.

GPT-5.2.name: Gareth Lewis
GPT-5.2.story:
Gareth sat down at the feast table and immediately looked at the dishes with a frown. “Is this it?” he said, loud enough for people nearby to hear. When a plate was placed in front of him, he poked at the food with his fork and said, “Why is everything so soft?”

Someone offered him fish, and Gareth shook his head. “I don’t eat that,” he said, then added, “Do you have anything normal?” He stood up without waiting and walked over to the serving area, lifting lids off pots. “What’s in here?” he asked, peering in.

When a host tried to guide him back, Gareth waved a hand. “I’ll just grab what I want,” he said. He took a spoon from a tray and scooped a large serving onto his plate, spilling sauce onto the table.

As a man spoke to the group, Gareth interrupted, “Can we hurry up? I’ve got places to be.” A few people stopped eating and looked toward him. Gareth pulled out his phone, scrolled, and laughed at something on the screen.

He took a bite, grimaced, and said, “This is weird,” then pushed his plate away. When music started, he called out, “Turn it down,” and clapped once in an exaggerated way. He stood again, stepped between seated guests, and said, “I’m going to find a shop,” then walked off past the edge of the tables without saying goodbye to the hosts.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When joining a group, the newcomer shakes hands with everyone present and introduces themself clearly.

GPT-5.2.name: Peni Waqanivavalagi
GPT-5.2.story:
Peni arrived at the community hall just as people were gathering around a long table with clipboards and a thermos of tea. He paused at the doorway, wiped his palms on his shorts, and stepped in.

“Bula,” someone said from the far end.

Peni walked straight to the nearest person, a man in a faded rugby jersey, and held out his hand. “Bula. I’m Peni Waqanivavalagi,” he said.

The man shook firmly. “Ratu. Bula, Peni.”

Peni moved to the next person, a woman stacking plastic cups. “Bula, I’m Peni,” he repeated, offering his hand.

She shook once and smiled. “Sera.”

He continued down the line, making sure he reached each person: two older men by the window, a young guy with a notebook, and a woman holding a toddler on her hip. Each time, he introduced himself clearly, waited for their name, and answered with a short “Nice to meet you” before stepping on.

When he reached the end, he turned back to the group. “Thanks, everyone. I’m here to help with the clean-up roster,” he said, then picked up an extra chair and slid it quietly into the circle.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: If health constraints make handshakes risky, the newcomer explains briefly, offers a warm verbal greeting to each person, and uses an appropriate non-contact gesture.

GPT-5.2.name: Laisa Vunibobo
GPT-5.2.story:
Laisa arrived at the small office in Lautoka where the volunteer group met, a stack of flyers tucked under her arm. Three people stood near the whiteboard, talking. As she stepped in, one of them moved forward with a hand extended.

Laisa lifted her own hands slightly and smiled. “Bula. Sorry—I’m not doing handshakes today,” she said, keeping her voice calm. “I’ve got a health thing and I’m being careful.”

The person stopped and nodded. “All good.”

Laisa turned to each person in order, making sure she faced them. She gave a small wave with her fingers and a short bow of her head. “Bula, I’m Laisa Vunibobo,” she said to the first. “Nice to meet you.”

“Joeli,” he replied.

She repeated it with the next two—“Bula, I’m Laisa”—and each time used the same non-contact gesture, keeping a respectful distance.

When everyone had answered with their names, Laisa set the flyers on the table. “Thanks for understanding,” she said. “I can help with delivery routes and the sign-up sheet.”

A marker squeaked as someone uncapped it, and Laisa pulled a chair back to join the circle.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The newcomer shakes hands quickly but avoids eye contact and does not introduce themself, making the gesture feel hollow.

GPT-5.2.name: Owen Hart
GPT-5.2.story:
Owen entered the workshop space behind the hardware store in Labasa where a few people were gathered around a table of tools. A man stepped forward and offered his hand.

Owen clasped it for a second, eyes already drifting to the shelves. He released and immediately grabbed the next hand that appeared.

“Bula,” a woman said, smiling.

Owen’s mouth twitched. “Yeah,” he replied, giving a rapid shake, then turned his shoulders away before she could ask anything. He moved down the line, taking hands one after another, fingers cool and quick, never holding the handshake long enough for anyone to settle into it.

“What’s your name, brother?” someone asked.

Owen shrugged and kept looking over their heads. He gave another fast shake, then another, nodding vaguely without meeting anyone’s eyes.

A man holding a tape measure paused with his hand half-extended. Owen took it, pumped once, and let go.

“Okay,” the man said, drawing his hand back.

Owen dropped into a chair at the end of the table without speaking further. He pulled out his phone, scrolled once, and then looked up at the tools again while the others resumed their conversation.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When joining a group, the newcomer sits down without greeting anyone and begins talking as if they are already included.

GPT-5.2.name: Kyla Bennett
GPT-5.2.story:
Kyla pushed through the door of the netball club room and went straight to an empty chair at the table. People were mid-conversation, leaning over a printed fixture list. Kyla dropped her tote on the floor and sat down.

She pulled out a pen and said, “Okay, so what time are we starting Saturday? Because I can’t do early.”

A woman across from her paused, hand still holding a stapler. “Uh—bula,” the woman said slowly.

Kyla didn’t look up. “Also, we should change the warm-up. The last one was too long,” she added, tapping the paper in front of her as if it belonged to her.

Two men at the end of the table exchanged a glance. Someone cleared their throat.

Kyla continued, “And who’s bringing water? I’m not doing it. I did it last time.”

A young player shifted in her seat and said, “Sorry, what’s your name again?”

Kyla shrugged. “I’m here now,” she replied, flipping the fixture list to the back page.

The room went quieter for a moment, then a coach leaned forward and began reintroducing the agenda, while Kyla kept her pen poised and started writing on the margin.
